question: [File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/transaction/RecordItem.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: parseParent()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a child transaction whose parentConsensusTimestamp matches previous.parent.transactionRecord.getParentConsensusTimestamp() (the grandparent path at line 340) to force a RecordItem to adopt an incorrect grandparent, causing downstream history to record a false parent-child lineage in the mirror node database?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/transaction/RecordItem.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: parseParent()] Can an unprivileged user submit a transaction with a parentConsensusTimestamp that does not match any of the three checked positions (previous, previous.parent, previous.parent.parent) so that parseParent() silently falls back to the injected this.parent field, allowing a caller-supplied parent reference to override the stream-derived parent and corrupt the recorded transaction tree?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/transaction/RecordItem.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: parseParent()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the fact that parseParent() only traverses three levels deep (previous, previous.parent, previous.parent.parent) so that a legitimate child transaction with a valid parentConsensusTimestamp pointing to a great-grandparent is silently assigned a null parent, causing the mirror node to record it as a top-level transaction and breaking the historical transaction tree?
